problem: Imagine you're drinking water out of a cup through a straw. What is the maximum theoretical length of the straw that allows you to suck the water up, even if you had a vacuum pump?
solution: The largest possible pressure differential you can make is $P_0$, so this equals $\rho_wgh$. Thus $h=P_0/\rho_wg=\boxed{10.3 \, \unit{m}}$.

The fluid is \fbox{not stable}, because the greater density on the right means a greater pressure existing on the right, leading fluid parcels along the interface to move toward the left. After a long time, all the heavier fluid will end up on the bottom as to minimize the gravitational potential energy on the system.

problem:
\label{bub}
    Imagine enclosing the surface $S$ in Theorem \ref{tip1} with a portion of area $A$ along the bottom to make a closed shell. Now, fill the shell with a fluid with pressure $P$. By using Newton's laws in the $z$ direction, explain why the total force in the $z$ direction on the surface $S$ has magnitude $PA$.
solution: By Newton's third law, the total force exerted by the gas on the shell must be 0, so the shell cannot move. We can easily see that the force the gas exerts on the bottom of area $A$ is $PA.$ As a result, for the total force to be 0 on the entire shell, the force the gas exerts on the surface $S$ must also be $\boxed{PA}$.

Using the hint, we know that since $B$ is on the interface of the water and air on a flat surface, the pressure is $\boxed{P_B=P_0}$ there. The pressures at $A$ and $C$ follow easily with $\boxed{P_A=P_0-\rho gh}$ and $\boxed{P_C=P_0+\rho gH}$.

problem: As an extension of the previous problem, let a sphere of water have radius $r$ and surface tension $\gamma$. The force of surface tension is found as $\gamma \ell$, where $\ell$ is the length of the interface between two mediums. Find the pressure in the sphere as a function of $r$, $\gamma$, and $P_0$. Hint: Split the sphere in half and use force balance.
solution: If we split the sphere in half, we consider the forces along the symmetry axis of the sphere. The net force due to pressure on the sphere is due to that acting on its hemispherical surface. There are two components to this -- the outward pressure from the water and the inward pressure from the air. Using Theorem \ref{tip1}, the net force is simply the pressure differential times the area of the projection of the hemisphere onto a flat surface, which is just a circle of radius $r$. Hence, the net force due to pressure is $(P-P_0)\pi r^2$. This net force is balanced by the force of surface tension, which acts along the circumference of the circular base of the sphere: $2\pi r\gamma$. Equating these two forces, we get $$(P-P_0)\pi r^2=2\pi r\gamma\Rightarrow P=\boxed{P_0+\frac{2\gamma}{r}}$$

problem: Let's say you have a scale with some fixed relative uncertainty $\sigma$. Approximate the value of $\sigma$ such that if you measure your weight in the atmosphere versus in a vacuum, you'll be able to resolve the difference in weight using the scale. In other words, what is the minimum $\sigma$ to be able to say the difference in your measurements is statistically significant?
solution: Since the human body is made mostly of water, we approximate your body density to be about 1000 times greater than air. Then, if your mass is $m$, your weighted as measured in a vacuum differs from your weight in air by an amount $(\rho_a/\rho_w)m\approx 0.001m$. The relative error must then be half of the factor $\rho_a/\rho_w$ for us to resolve the two measurements, so $\sigma\approx \boxed{0.0005}.$

problem:
A U-tube manometer consists of a uniform diameter cylindrical tube that is bent into a U shape. It is originally filled with water. The total length of the column of water is $L$. Ignore surface tension and viscosity. Oil with a density half that of water is added to one side of the tube until the total length of oil is equal to the total length of water. Determine the equilibrium height difference between the two sides.
    \begin{enumerate}[label = (\Alph*)]
\item $L$
\item $L/2$
\item $L/3$
\item $3L/4$
\item $L/4$
\end{enumerate}
solution: Let the length of water on the side of the tube with pure water be $h$ after the oil is added. The pressures at the bottom of each section of the tube must be the same so that the fluid parcels feel no force acting on them. This means that $$\rho gh=\frac\rho 2gL+\rho g(L-h)$$which solves to yield $h=3L/4$. The height of fluid in the other side is then $L+L-h=5L/4$, so the difference in height is $\textbf{(B)}$ $L/2$.

problem: We define the \textit{momentum density} $\rho_p$ of a fluid to be the momentum per unit volume of a fluid. For a fluid of density $\rho$ and a speed $v$ at some point, find $\rho_p$.
solution: We have $$\rho_p=\frac{mv}{V}=\boxed{\rho v}$$

problem: Water with pressure $P_1$ is flowing through a pipe of cross-sectional area $A_1$ at a speed $v_1$ before the pipe narrows into a cross-sectional area $A_2$ without loss of energy. Find the new pressure of the water $P_2$.
solution: By Bernoulli's law, $$P_1+\frac12\rho v_1^2=P_2+\frac12\rho v_2^2$$By continuity, $v_2=(A_1/A_2)v_1$. Combining both equations, we get $$P_2=\boxed{P_1+\frac12\rho v_1^2\left(1-\frac{A_1^2}{A_2^2}\right)}$$

problem:
A hose with gauge pressure $P$, cross sectional area $A$, and water velocity $v$ is fed into a sprinkler that narrows the stream into a cross-sectional area $S$ as it spits out the water into the air. \begin{anum}\item  Find the change in the kinetic energy per unit volume of the water between when it is in the hose and when it is just released into the air by the sprinkler.
    \item Find the work done by external forces per unit volume of the water between the same two points discussed in part (a).
    \item Find the energy lost to frictional forces per unit volume of water. 
    \item What must $P$ be so that the energy lost in part (c) equals 0? You'll see that this matches Bernoulli's law.
    \end{anum}

solution: \fbox{Alice is correct}. The issue with Bob's logic is that he forgot the force that the water exerts on the walls of the cup. If the walls are not slanted, the force exerted on the walls is perfectly horizontal. If the walls are slanted inward like in the middle cup, the water exerts an upward force on the cup that perfectly balances out the increased downward force on the base of the cup. If the walls are slanted outward like in the right cup, the water exerts an additional downward force along with the force on the base. As a result, the total force exerted on the scale by the cup is the same in all cases.

problem: A car is turning left along a circular track of radius $r$ at a constant speed $v$. A cylindrical beaker is placed vertically inside the car. The beaker has a small hole on its right side. If the water's highest point in the beaker is a height $h$ above the hole, at what instantaneous speed does the water escape the hole, from a passenger's perspective?

solution:
The pressure force only depends on the height of the water, so it would equal the weight of the water in the container if we replaced the block with water. The block is half as dense as water, so the total mass of water would be $(M+2m)g$. We still need to include the force from the atmosphere, so we get \textbf{(E)} $P_{\text{atm}}A+(M+2m)g$. \\

    We can also find the answer from force balance. The net force acting on the bottom of the container is $P_{\text{atm}}A+(M+m)g$. The tension in the string keeps the block in equilibrium, and exerts a force $mg$ downward on the block to balance the buoyant force of $2mg$. This tension thus creates an upward force of $mg$ ont he base of the container. Since the total force equals the tension plus the pressure force, we again get \textbf{(E)} $P_{\text{atm}}A+(M+2m)g$.

Because of buoyancy, the net force on the bucket while it's fully submerged is 0. As the bucket begins to rise out of the water, the force linearly increases until the entire bucket is out of the water, at which point the force is $F=\rho g\pi R^2h$. The average force exerted on the bucket during this process is thus $\bar F=\rho g\pi R^2h/2$, and the total work is then $\bar Fh=\rho g\pi R^2h^2/2$, answer choice \textbf{(D)}.

The total weight of the water is $$F_g=\rho La^2g\frac{\sqrt 3}{4}$$This weight is due to the vertical component of pressure acting on each half of the trough, so we conclude that the vertical component of force acting on each half of the trough is $F_y=F_g/2$. Since the pressure force is directed perpendicular to the faces of the trough, the horizontal component force acting on each half of the trough is related to the vertical force by $F_y/F_x=\tan (30^\circ)$. As a result, $$F_x=\frac{F_y}{\tan(30^\circ)}=\frac{F_g\sqrt3}{2}=\boxed{\frac{3}{8}\rho La^2g}$$By force balance, this force is exactly equal to the force exerted by the tape.

problem: A box of volume $V$ contains gas molecules with mass $m$ and number density $n$ (i.e. there are $n$ molecules per unit volume) at a very low temperature (this means the gas particles are nearly completely still relative to the box). The box originally moves with a constant speed $v$, and suddenly stops. Find the pressure $P$ on the front face of the box as soon as the box stops moving.
solution: Let $A$ be the area of the front face of the box. When the box stops moving, the gas molecules keep moving with speed $v$. In a small period of time $\Delta t$, there will be $N=nAv\Delta t$ molecules colliding with the wall. Each of these molecules will exert an impulse $\Delta p=2mv$ as they bounce off elastically. The total impulse is thus $N\Delta p$, and the force on the front face will be $$F=\frac{N\Delta p}{\Delta t}=\frac{2nmAv^2\Delta t}{\Delta t}=2nmAv^2$$The pressure is then $$P=\frac{F}{A}=\boxed{2nmv^2}$$

When the container is full, the water level is at a height $R$ so the pressure along the bottom of the container is $P=\rho gR$. The total force exerted by the water on the ground is thus $F=P\pi R^2=\pi \rho gR^3$. By Newton's third law, this must be the total normal force exerted upward on the system by the ground. For the container to just begin lifting up at this point, the normal force must equal the combined mass of the water and the container. As a result, $$\pi \rho gR^3=\frac23\pi\rho gR^3+M\Rightarrow M=\boxed{\frac13\pi\rho gR^3}$$

problem: If a beam with square cross-section and very low density is placed in water, it will sit in the water with one pair of its long opposite faces horizontal. This orientation, however, becomes unstable as we increase its density. Find the critical density when this transition occurs, in terms of the density of water $\rho$.
solution: See solution 15 at \href{https://physoly.tech/static/files/KaldaMech-121.pdf}{this link}.
